2, bit boundaries, etc.\n15\t\n16\tuse armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n17\tuse serde_yaml::Value;\n18\t\n19\t// ============================================================================\n20\t// Int32 Minimum Value Tests\n21\t// ============================================================================\n22\t\n23\t#[test]\n24\tfn test_int32_minimum_value() {\n25\t    /// Test: int32::MIN (-2147483648) cannot convert to uint32\n26\t    ///\n27\t    /// This is the absolute minimum value for a signed 32-bit integer.\n28\t    /// In two's complement representation, this is 0x80000000.\n29\t    ///\n30\t    /// # Why This Matters\n31\t    ///\n32\t    /// - int32::MIN is the most negative value representable in int32\n33\t    /// - It has a special binary representation (only the sign bit set)\n34\t    /// - Converting to uint32 would be an overflow/underflow condition\n35\t    let yaml = r#\"value: -2147483648\"#;\n36\t    let value: Result<Value, _> = serde_yaml::from_str(yaml);\n37\t\n38\t    assert!(value.is_ok(), \"YAML parsing should succeed for int32::MIN\");\n39\t\n40\t    let value = value.unwrap();\n41\t    let field_value = &value[\"value\"];\n42\t\n43\t    // Verify it's parsed as a negative integer\n44\t    assert!(\n45\t        field_value.is_i64(),\n46\t        \"int32::MIN should be parsed as i64\"\n47\t    );\n48\t    let int_value = field_value.as_i64().unwrap();\n49\t    assert_eq!(int_value, -2147483648, \"Value should be int32::MIN\");\n50\t    assert!(int_value < 0, \"int32::MIN is negative\");\n51\t\n52\t    // Verify it would not fit in uint32 range\n53\t    let fits_in_uint32 = int_value >= 0 && int_value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n54\t    assert!(\n55\t        !fits_in_uint32,\n56\t        \"int32::MIN should not fit in uint32 range\"\n57\t    );\n58\t\n59\t    // Verify type mismatch error is properly created\n60\t    let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"value\", \"uint32\", \"int32_min\");\n61\t    assert!(\n62\t        error.is_type_mismatch(),\n63\t        \"Type mismatch error should be created for int32::MIN\"\n64\t    );\n65\t\n66\t    let error_msg = format!(\"{}\", error.kind);\n67\t    assert!(\n68\t        error_msg.contains(\"uint32\") || error_msg.contains(\"unsigned\"),\n69\t        \"Error should mention uint32/unsigned type\"\n70\t    );\n71\t\n72\t    println!(\"✓ int32::MIN (-2147483648) properly rejected for uint32 conversion\");\n73\t}\n74\t\n75\t#[test]\n76\tfn test_int32_minimum_plus_variations() {\n77\t    /// Test: Values near int32::MIN\n78\t    ///\n79\t    /// Test values just above int32::MIN to ensure boundary detection works.\n80\t    ///\n81\t    /// # Test Cases\n82\t    ///\n83\t    /// | Input | Description |\n84\t    /// |-------|-------------|\n85\t    /// | -2147483648 | int32::MIN (exact) |\n86\t    /// | -2147483647 | int32::MIN + 1 |\n87\t    /// | -2147483640 | int32::MIN + 8 |\n88\t    /// | -2147483638 | int32::MIN + 10 |\n89\t    /// | -2147483500 | int32::MIN + 148 |\n90\t    /// | -2147483000 | int32::MIN + 648 |\n91\t    /// | -2147480000 | int32::MIN + 3648 |\n92\t    let test_cases = vec![\n93\t        (-2147483648i64, \"int32::MIN exact\"),\n94\t        (-2147483647i64, \"int32::MIN + 1\"),\n95\t        (-2147483640i64, \"int32::MIN + 8\"),\n96\t        (-2147483638i64, \"int32::MIN + 10\"),\n97\t        (-2147483500i64, \"int32::MIN + 148\"),\n98\t        (-2147483000i64, \"int32::MIN + 648\"),\n99\t        (-2147480000i64, \"int32::MIN + 3648\"),\n100\t    ];\n101\t\n102\t    for (expected_value, description) in test_cases {\n103\t        let yaml = format!(\"value: {}\", expected_value);\n104\t        let value: Result<Value, _> = serde_yaml::from_str(&yaml);\n105\t\n106\t        assert!(\n107\t            value.is_ok(),\n108\t            \"YAML parsing should succeed for {}\",\n109\t            description\n110\t        );\n111\t\n112\t        let value = value.unwrap();\n113\t        let field_value = &value[\"value\"];\n114\t\n115\t        // Verify it's a negative integer\n116\t        assert!(\n117\t            field_value.is_i64(),\n118\t            \"Value should be i64 for {}\",\n119\t            description\n120\t        );\n121\t        let int_value = field_value.as_i64().unwrap();\n122\t        assert_eq!(\n123\t            int_value, expected_value,\n124\t            \"Value should match expected for {}\",\n125\t            description\n126\t        );\n127\t        assert!(int_value < 0, \"Value should be negative for {}\", description);\n128\t\n129\t        // Verify it would not fit in uint32\n130\t        let fits_in_uint32 = int_value >= 0 && int_value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n131\t        assert!(\n132\t            !fits_in_uint32,\n133\t            \"Value {} should not fit in uint32 ({})\",\n134\t            expected_value, description\n135\t        );\n136\t\n137\t        // Verify type mismatch error\n138\t        let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"value\", \"uint32\", \"int32_negative\");\n139\t        assert!(\n140\t            error.is_type_mismatch(),\n141\t            \"Type mismatch error should be created for {}\",\n142\t            description\n143\t        );\n144\t\n145\t        println!(\"✓ {} ({}): properly rejected\", expected_value, description);\n146\t    }\n147\t\n148\t    println!(\"✓ All int32::MIN variations properly handled\");\n149\t}\n150\t\n151\t// ============================================================================\n152\t// Maximum Negative Values (Closest to Zero)\n153\t// ============================================================================\n154\t\n155\t#[test]\n156\tfn test_maximum_negative_values() {\n157\t    /// Test: Maximum negative values (closest to zero)\n158\t    ///\n159\t    /// These are the \"largest\" negative integers in the mathematical sense\n160\t    /// (closest to zero, but still negative).\n161\t    ///\n162\t    /// # Test Cases\n163\t    ///\n164\t    /// | Input | Description | Distance from Zero |\n165\t    /// |-------|-------------|-------------------|\n166\t    /// | -1 | Largest negative value | 1 |\n167\t    /// | -2 | Second largest negative | 2 |\n168\t    /// | -3 | Third largest negative | 3 |\n169\t    /// | -10 | Small negative magnitude | 10 |\n170\t    /// | -100 | Century boundary | 100 |\n171\t    /// | -128 | Power of 2 boundary | 128 |\n172\t    /// | -255 | Byte boundary - 1 | 255 |\n173\t    /// | -256 | Byte boundary | 256 |\n174\t    let test_cases = vec![\n175\t        (-1i64, \"negative one (closest to zero)\"),\n176\t        (-2i64, \"negative two\"),\n177\t        (-3i64, \"negative three\"),\n178\t        (-5i64, \"negative five\"),\n179\t        (-10i64, \"negative ten\"),\n180\t        (-15i64, \"negative fifteen\"),\n181\t        (-16i64, \"negative sixteen (2^4)\"),\n182\t        (-31i64, \"negative thirty-one\"),\n183\t        (-32i64, \"negative thirty-two (2^5)\"),\n184\t        (-63i64, \"negative sixty-three\"),\n185\t        (-64i64, \"negative sixty-four (2^6)\"),\n186\t        (-100i64, \"negative hundred\"),\n187\t        (-127i64, \"negative 127 (2^7 - 1)\"),\n188\t        (-128i64, \"negative 128 (2^7)\"),\n189\t        (-255i64, \"negative 255 (2^8 - 1)\"),\n190\t        (-256i64, \"negative 256 (2^8)\"),\n191\t        (-512i64, \"negative 512 (2^9)\"),\n192\t        (-1000i64, \"negative thousand\"),\n193\t        (-1024i64, \"negative 1024 (2^10)\"),\n194\t    ];\n195\t\n196\t    for (expected_value, description) in test_cases {\n197\t        let yaml = format!(\"value: {}\", expected_value);\n198\t        let value: Result<Value, _> = serde_yaml::from_str(&yaml);\n199\t\n200\t        assert!(\n201\t            value.is_ok(),\n202\t            \"YAML parsing should succeed for {}\",\n203\t            description\n204\t        );\n205\t\n206\t        let value = value.unwrap();\n207\t        let field_value = &value[\"value\"];\n208\t\n209\t        // Verify it's a negative integer\n210\t        assert!(\n211\t            field_value.is_i64(),\n212\t            \"Value should be i64 for {}\",\n213\t            description\n214\t        );\n215\t        let int_value = field_value.as_i64().unwrap();\n216\t        assert_eq!(\n217\t            int_value, expected_value,\n218\t            \"Value should match expected for {}\",\n219\t            description\n220\t        );\n221\t        assert!(int_value < 0, \"Value should be negative for {}\", description);\n222\t\n223\t        // All negative values, even those close to zero, cannot convert to uint32\n224\t        let fits_in_uint32 = int_value >= 0 && int_value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n225\t        assert!(\n226\t            !fits_in_uint32,\n227\t            \"Negative value {} should not fit in uint32 ({})\",\n228\t            expected_value, description\n229\t        );\n230\t\n231\t        // Verify type mismatch error\n232\t        let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"value\", \"uint32\", \"int32_negative\");\n233\t        assert!(\n234\t            error.is_type_mismatch(),\n235\t            \"Type println!(\"✓ {} ({}): properly rejected as negative\", expected_value, description);\n247\t    }\n248\t\n249\t    println!(\"✓ All maximum negative values (closest to zero) properly handled\");\n250\t}\n251\t\n252\t#[test]\n253\tfn test_negative_one_special_case() {\n254\t    /// Test: -1 as a special boundary case\n255\t    ///\n256\t    /// -1 is particularly important because:\n257\t    /// 1. It's the closest negative integer to zero\n258\t    /// 2. In two's complement, it's all 1s (0xFFFFFFFF for 32-bit)\n259\t    /// 3. When incorrectly cast to unsigned, it becomes UINT32_MAX\n260\t    /// 4. This is a very common bug in C/C++ code\n261\t    let yaml = r#\"value: -1\"#;\n262\t    let value: Result<Value, _> = serde_yaml::from_str(yaml);\n263\t\n264\t    assert!(value.is_ok(), \"YAML parsing should succeed for -1\");\n265\t\n266\t    let value = value.unwrap();\n267\t    let field_value = &value[\"value\"];\n268\t\n269\t    // Verify it's -1\n270\t    assert!(field_value.is_i64(), \"-1 should be parsed as i64\");\n271\t    let int_value = field_value.as_i64().unwrap();\n272\t    assert_eq!(int_value, -1, \"Value should be exactly -1\");\n273\t    assert!(int_value < 0, \"-1 is negative\");\n274\t\n275\t    // Important: -1 does NOT fit in uint32\n276\t    // (Even though some buggy code might incorrectly cast it)\n277\t    let fits_in_uint32 = int_value >= 0 && int_value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n278\t    assert!(\n279\t        !fits_in_uint32,\n280\t        \"-1 should not be considered valid for uint32\"\n281\t    );\n282\t\n283\t    // Verify type mismatch error\n284\t    let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"value\", \"uint32\", \"negative_one\");\n285\t    assert!(\n286\t        error.is_type_mismatch(),\n287\t        \"Type mismatch error should be created for -1\"\n288\t    );\n289\t\n290\t    let error_msg = format!(\"{}\", error.kind);\n291\t    assert!(\n292\t        error_msg.contains(\"uint32\") || error_msg.contains(\"unsigned\"),\n293\t        \"Error should mention uint32/unsigned\"\n294\t    );\n295\t    assert!(\n296\t        error_msg.contains(\"negative\") || error_msg.contains(\"-1\"),\n297\t        \"Error should indicate negative value\"\n298\t    );\n299\t\n300\t    println!(\"✓ -1 properly rejected (critical boundary case)\");\n301\t}\n302\t\n303\t// ============================================================================\n304\t// Zero Boundary Cases\n305\t// ============================================================================\n306\t\n307\t#[test]\n308\tfn test_zero_boundary_transition() {\n309\t    /// Test: Zero boundary and transition from negative to non-negative\n310\t    ///\n311\t    /// This test verifies the behavior at the exact boundary between\n312\t    /// negative and non-negative values.\n313\t    ///\n314\t    /// # Test Cases\n315\t    ///\n316\t    /// | Input | Sign | Can be uint32? | Description |\n317\t    /// |-------|------|----------------|-------------|\n318\t    /// | -1 | Negative | NO | Last negative value |\n319\t    /// | 0 | Zero | YES | First non-negative value |\n320\t    /// | 1 | Positive | YES | First positive value |\n321\t    let test_cases = vec![\n322\t        (-1i64, \"negative\", false, \"last negative before zero\"),\n323\t        (0i64, \"zero\", true, \"exact zero (boundary)\"),\n324\t        (1i64, \"positive\", true, \"first positive after zero\"),\n325\t    ];\n326\t\n327\t    for (value, sign_description, should_fit_uint32, description) in test_cases {\n328\t        let yaml = format!(\"value: {}\", value);\n329\t        let parsed: Result<Value, _> = serde_yaml::from_str(&yaml);\n330\t\n331\t        assert!(\n332\t            parsed.is_ok(),\n333\t            \"YAML parsing should succeed for {}\",\n334\t            description\n335\t        );\n336\t\n337\t        let parsed = parsed.unwrap();\n338\t        let field_value = &parsed[\"value\"];\n339\t\n340\t        // Verify it's parsed as integer\n341\t        assert!(\n342\t            field_value.is_i64(),\n343\t            \"Value should be i64 for {}\",\n344\t            description\n345\t        );\n346\t        let int_value = field_value.as_i64().unwrap();\n347\t        assert_eq!(int_value, value, \"Value should match expected\");\n348\t\n349\t        // Check if fits in uint32\n350\t        let fits_in_uint32 = int_value >= 0 && int_value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n351\t        assert_eq!(\n352\t            fits_in_uint32, should_fit_uint32,\n353\t            \"Value {} should {}fit in uint32 ({})\",\n354\t            value,\n355\t            if should_fit_uint32 { \"\" } else { \"not \" },\n356\t            description\n357\t        );\n358\t\n359\t        // For negative values, verify error handling\n360\t        if !should_fit_uint32 {\n361\t            let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"value\", \"uint32\", \"int32_negative\");\n362\t            assert!(\n363\t                error.is_type_mismatch(),\n364\t                \"Type mismatch error should be created for {}\",\n365\t                description\n366\t            );\n367\t\n368\t            let error_msg = format!(\"{}\", error.kind);\n369\t            assert!(\n370\t                error_msg.contains(\"uint32\") || error_msg.contains(\"unsigned\"),\n371\t                \"Error should mention uint32 for {}\",\n372\t                description\n373\t            );\n374\t        }\n375\t\n376\t        println!(\"✓ {}: {} sign, uint32 fit={}\", value, sign_description, fits_in_uint32);\n377\t    }\n378\t\n379\t    println!(\"✓ Zero boundary transition properly handled\");\n380\t}\n381\t\n382\t#[test]\n383\tfn test_zero_with_negative_context() {\n384\t    /// Test: Zero in context where negative was expected\n385\t    ///\n386\t    /// This tests the edge case where a field might conceptually be\n387\t    /// related to negative values, but zero is technically valid.\n388\t    let yaml = r#\"value: 0\"#;\n389\t    let value: Result<Value, _> = serde_yaml::from_str(yaml);\n390\t\n391\t    assert!(value.is_ok(), \"YAML parsing should succeed for 0\");\n392\t\n393\t    let value = value.unwrap();\n394\t    let field_value = &value[\"value\"];\n395\t\n396\t    // Verify it's zero\n397\t    assert!(field_value.is_i64(), \"0 should be parsed as i64\");\n398\t    let int_value = field_value.as_i64().unwrap();\n399\t    assert_eq!(int_value, 0, \"Value should be exactly 0\");\n400\t\n401\t    // Zero IS valid for uint32 (it's in the range [0, UINT32_MAX])\n402\t    let fits_in_uint32 = int_value >= 0 && int_value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n403\t    assert!(\n404\t        fits_in_uint32,\n405\t        \"Zero should fit in uint32 range\"\n406\t    );\n407\t\n408\t    // Verify no type mismatch error for zero (it's valid!)\n409\t    // We only test error creation for actual negative values\n410\t\n411\t    println!(\"✓ Zero is valid for uint32 (boundary case handled correctly)\");\n412\t}\n413\t\n414\t// Test: Negative values at power of 2 boundaries\n531\t    ///\n532\t    /// These test values at powers of 2 and adjacent values to test\n533\t    /// boundary detection around bit-width transitions.\n534\t    ///\n535\t    /// # Test Cases\n536\t    ///\n537\t    /// Powers of 2 and their negatives:\n538\t    /// - 2^0 = 1, test -1\n539\t    /// - 2^1 = 2, test -2\n540\t    /// - 2^2 = 4, test -4\n541\t    /// - ...\n542\t    /// - 2^16 = 65536, test -65536\n543\t    /// - 2^20 = 1048576, test -1048576\n544\t    /// - 2^30 = 1073741824, test -1073741824\n545\t    /// - 2^31 = 2147483648, test -2147483648 (int32::MIN)\n546\t    let test_cases = vec![\n547\t        (-1i64, \"-2^0\", \"negative 1 (2^0)\"),\n548\t        (-2i64, \"-2^1\", \"negative 2 (2^1)\"),\n549\t test_negative_values_near_uint32_boundaries() {\n705\t    /// Test: Negative values that would overflow uint32 boundaries\n706\t    ///\n707\t    /// These tests verify that negative values are properly rejected\n708\t    /// even when their absolute value might be within uint32 range.\n709\t    ///\n710\t    /// The key insight: For uint32, the valid range is [0, 4294967295].\n711\t    /// ANY negative value is invalid, regardless of its magnitude.\n712\t    ///\n713\t    /// # Test Cases\n714\t    ///\n715\t    /// | Input | Absolute Value | Would fit as uint32 | Should Reject? |\n716\t    /// |-------|----------------|-------------------|----------------|\n717\t    /// | -1 | 1 | Yes (as 1) | YES (it's negative!) |\n718\t    /// | -100 | 100 | Yes (as 100) | YES (it's negative!) |\n719\t    /// | -1000 | 1000 | Yes (as 1000) | YES (it's negative!) |\n720\t    /// | -1000000 | 1000000 | Yes | YES (it's negative!) |\n721\t    /// | -1000000000 | 1000000000 | Yes | YES (it's negative!) |\n722\t    let test_cases = vec![\n723\t        (-1i64, 1u64, \"absolute value 1\"),\n724\t        (-100i64, 100u64, \"absolute value 100\"),\n725\t        (-1000i64, 1000u64, \"absolute value 1000\"),\n726\t        (-10000i64, 10000u64, \"absolute value 10000\"),\n727\t        (-100000i64, 100000u64, \"absolute value 100000\"),\n728\t        (-1000000i64, 1000000u64, \"absolute value 1000000\"),\n729\t        (-10000000i64, 10000000u64, \"absolute value 10000000\"),\n730\t        (-100000000i64, 100000000u64, \"absolute value 100000000\"),\n731\t        (-1000000000i64, 1000000000u64, \"absolute value 1000000000\"),\n732\t    ];\n733\t\n734\t    for (negative_value, absolute_value, description) in test_cases {\n735\t        let yaml = format!(\"value: {}\", negative_value);\n736\t        let value: Result<Value, _> = serde_yaml::from_str(&yaml);\n737\t\n738\t        assert!(\n739\t            value.is_ok(),\n740\t            \"YAML parsing should succeed for {}\",\n741\t            description\n742\t        );\n743\t\n744\t        let value = value.unwrap();\n745\t        let field_value = &value[\"value\"];\n746\t\n747\t        // Verify it's a negative integer\n748\t        assert!(\n749\t            field_value.is_i64(),\n750\t            \"Value should be i64 for {}\",\n751\t            description\n752\t        );\n753\t        let int_value = field_value.as_i64().unwrap();\n754\t        assert_eq!(int_value, negative_value, \"Value should be negative\");\n755\t\n756\t        // The absolute value WOULD fit in uint32...\n757\t        assert!(\n758\t            absolute_value <= u32::MAX as u64,\n759\t            \"Absolute value should fit in uint32 for {}\",\n760\t            description\n761\t        );\n762\t\n763\t        // ...but the negative value itself must NOT be accepted\n764\t        let fits_in_uint32 = int_value >= 0 && int_value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n765\t        assert!(\n766\t            !fits_in_uint32,\n767\t            \"Negative value {} should NOT fit in uint32 ({}) - sign matters!\",\n768\t            negative_value, description\n769\t        );\n770\t\n771\t        // Verify type mismatch error\n772\t        let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"value\", \"uint32\", \"int32_negative\");\n773\t        assert!(\n774\t            error.is_type_mismatch(),\n775\t            \"Type mismatch error should be created for {}\",\n776\t            description\n777\t        );\n778\t\n779\t        println!(\n780\t            \"✓ {} ({}): negative sign correctly prevents conversion\",\n781\t            negative_value, description\n782\t        );\n783\t    }\n784\t\n785\t    println!(\"✓ All negative values properly rejected (sign check works correctly)\");\n786\t}\n787\t\n788\t// ============================================================================\n789\t// Summary and Coverage Verification\n790\t// ============================================================================\n791\t\n792\t#[test]\n793\tfn test_int32_to_uint32_boundary_coverage_summary() {\n794\t    /// Test: Verify comprehensive coverage of all boundary conditions\n795\t    ///\n796\t    /// This test verifies that we have covered all the critical boundary\n797\t    /// conditions for int32 to uint32 negative conversion.\n798\t    ///\n799\t    /// # Coverage Checklist\n800\t    ///\n801\t    /// - [x] int32::MIN (-2147483648) - absolute minimum\n802\t    /// - [x] Maximum negative values (-1, -2, -3, etc.) - closest to zero\n803\t    /// - [x] Zero boundary case (transition from -1 to 0 to 1)\n804\t    /// - [x] Range tests across all magnitudes\n805\t    /// - [x] Power-of-2 boundaries\n806\t    /// - [x] Common negative constants\n807\t    /// - [x] Values that would fit as unsigned but are negative\n808\t    ///\n809\t    /// This test documents the coverage and serves as a checklist for\n810\t    /// future maintenance.\n811\t\n812\t    // Document key boundary values\n813\t    let critical_values = vec![\n814\t        (\"int32::MIN\", -2147483648i64),\n815\t        (\"int32::MIN + 1\", -2147483647i64),\n816\t        (\"near int32::MIN\", -2000000000i64),\n817\t        (\"mid negative range\", -1000000000i64),\n818\t        (\"large negative\", -1000000i64),\n819\t        (\"medium negative\", -100000i64),\n820\t        (\"small negative\", -1000i64),\n821\t        (\"tiny negative\", -100i64),\n822\t        (\"negative one\", -1i64),\n823\t        (\"zero\", 0i64),\n824\t        (\"positive one\", 1i64),\n825\t    ];\n826\t\n827\t    println!(\"\\n=== Int32 to Uint32 Boundary Coverage Summary ===\\n\");\n828\t\n829\t    for (description, value) in critical_values {\n830\t        let is_negative = value < 0;\n831\t        let fits_uint32 = value >= 0 && value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n832\t        let should_reject = is_negative;\n833\t\n834\t        println!(\n835\t            \"{}: {} -> negative={}, fits_uint32={}, should_reject={}\",\n836\t            description, value, is_negative, fits_uint32, should_reject\n837\t        );\n838\t\n839\t        // Verify the logic is correct\n840\t        if should_reject {\n841\t            assert!(\n842\t                !fits_uint32 || value < 0,\n843\t                \"Rejected value should either not fit or be negative: {} ({})\",\n844\t                value, description\n845\t            );\n846\t        }\n847\t    }\n848\t\n849\t    println!(\"\\n✓ All boundary conditions documented and verified\");\n850\t    println!(\"✓ Coverage checklist complete\");\n851\t    println!(\"\\nKey Takeaways:\");\n852\t    println!(\"1. ALL negative int32 values are invalid for uint32 conversion\");\n853\t    println!(\"2. Zero (0) is valid - it's the boundary between valid/invalid\");\n854\t    println!(\"3. int32::MIN (-2147483648) is the extreme edge case\");\n855\t    println!(\"4. -1 is the 'closest' negative to zero, but still invalid\");\n856\t    println!(\"5. Magnitude doesn't matter - sign is the critical factor\");\n857\t}\n858\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"ad0e3261-71cd-462d-9c1e-4a06d7f1cdcf","uuid":"fb283b43-795a-476a-9616-3b62e554c40c","timestamp":"2026-07-13T13:41:58.328Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/ARMOR/tests/int32_to_uint32_boundary_test.rs","content":"//! Int32 to UInt32 Boundary Condition Tests\n//!\n//! Comprehensive edge case and boundary condition tests for int32 to uint32\n//! negative conversion. These tests ensure that all boundary values are properly\n//! handled and produce appropriate errors when negative int32 values cannot be\n//! converted to uint32.\n//!\n//! # Test Categories\n//!\n//! 1. **Int32 Minimum Value Tests** - Test i32::MIN (-2147483648) edge cases\n//! 2. **Maximum Negative Values** - Test values closest to zero (-1, -2, etc.)\n//! 3. **Zero Boundary Cases** - Test the transition between negative and positive\n//! 4. **Range Tests** - Test various magnitudes of negative values\n//! 5. **Special Boundary Values** - Powers of 2, bit boundaries, etc.\n\nuse armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\nuse serde_yaml::Value;\n\n// ============================================================================\n// Int32 Minimum Value Tests\n// ============================================================================\n\n#[test]\nfn test_int32_minimum_value() {\n    /// Test: int32::MIN (-2147483648) cannot convert to uint32\n    ///\n    /// This is the absolute minimum value for a signed 32-bit integer.\n    /// In two's complement representation, this is 0x80000000.\n    ///\n    /// # Why This Matters\n    ///\n    /// - int32::MIN is the most negative value representable in int32\n    /// - It has a special binary representation (only the sign bit set)\n    /// - Converting to uint32 would be an overflow/underflow condition\n    let yaml = r#\"value: -2147483648\"#;\n    let value: Result<Value, _> = serde_yaml::from_str(yaml);\n\n    assert!(value.is_ok(), \"YAML parsing should succeed for int32::MIN\");\n\n    let value = value.unwrap();\n    let field_value = &value[\"value\"];\n\n    // Verify it's parsed as a negative integer\n    assert!(\n        field_value.is_i64(),\n        \"int32::MIN should be parsed as i64\"\n    );\n    let int_value = field_value.as_i64().unwrap();\n    assert_eq!(int_value, -2147483648, \"Value should be int32::MIN\");\n    assert!(int_value < 0, \"int32::MIN is negative\");\n\n    // Verify it would not fit in uint32 range\n    let fits_in_uint32 = int_value >= 0 && int_value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n    assert!(\n        !fits_in_uint32,\n        \"int32::MIN should not fit in uint32 range\"\n    );\n\n    // Verify type mismatch error is properly created\n    let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"value\", \"uint32\", \"int32_min\");\n    assert!(\n        error.is_type_mismatch(),\n        \"Type mismatch error should be created for int32::MIN\"\n    );\n\n    let error_msg = format!(\"{}\", error.kind);\n    assert!(\n        error_msg.contains(\"uint32\") || error_msg.contains(\"unsigned\"),\n        \"Error should mention uint32/unsigned type\"\n    );\n\n    println!(\"✓ int32::MIN (-2147483648) properly rejected for uint32 conversion\");\n}\n\n#[test]\nfn test_int32_minimum_plus_variations() {\n    /// Test: Values near int32::MIN\n    ///\n    /// Test values just above int32::MIN to ensure boundary detection works.\n    ///\n    /// # Test Cases\n    ///\n    /// | Input | Description |\n    /// |-------|-------------|\n    /// | -2147483648 | int32::MIN (exact) |\n    /// | -2147483647 | int32::MIN + 1 |\n    /// | -2147483640 | int32::MIN + 8 |\n    /// | -2147483638 | int32::MIN + 10 |\n    /// | -2147483500 | int32::MIN + 148 |\n    /// | -2147483000 | int32::MIN + 648 |\n    /// | -2147480000 | int32::MIN + 3648 |\n    let test_cases = vec![\n        (-2147483648i64, \"int32::MIN exact\"),\n        (-2147483647i64, \"int32::MIN + 1\"),\n        (-2147483640i64, \"int32::MIN + 8\"),\n        (-2147483638i64, \"int32::MIN + 10\"),\n        (-2147483500i64, \"int32::MIN + 148\"),\n        (-2147483000i64, \"int32::MIN + 648\"),\n        (-2147480000i64, \"int32::MIN + 3648\"),\n    ];\n\n    for (expected_value, description) in test_cases {\n        let yaml = format!(\"value: {}\", expected_value);\n        let value: Result<Value, _> = serde_yaml::from_str(&yaml);\n\n        assert!(\n            value.is_ok(),\n            \"YAML parsing should succeed for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n\n        let value = value.unwrap();\n        let field_value = &value[\"value\"];\n\n        // Verify it's a negative integer\n        assert!(\n            field_value.is_i64(),\n            \"Value should be i64 for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n        let int_value = field_value.as_i64().unwrap();\n        assert_eq!(\n            int_value, expected_value,\n            \"Value should match expected for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n        assert!(int_value < 0, \"Value should be negative for {}\", description);\n\n        // Verify it would not fit in uint32\n        let fits_in_uint32 = int_value >= 0 && int_value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n        assert!(\n            !fits_in_uint32,\n            \"Value {} should not fit in uint32 ({})\",\n            expected_value, description\n        );\n\n        // Verify type mismatch error\n        let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"value\", \"uint32\", \"int32_negative\");\n        assert!(\n            error.is_type_mismatch(),\n            \"Type mismatch error should be created for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n\n        println!(\"✓ {} ({}): properly rejected\", expected_value, description);\n    }\n\n    println!(\"✓ All int32::MIN variations properly handled\");\n}\n\n// ============================================================================\n// Maximum Negative Values (Closest to Zero)\n// ============================================================================\n\n#[test]\nfn test_maximum_negative_values() {\n    /// Test: Maximum negative values (closest to zero)\n    ///\n    /// These are the \"largest\" negative integers in the mathematical sense\n    /// (closest to zero, but still negative).\n    ///\n    /// # Test Cases\n    ///\n    /// | Input | Description | Distance from Zero |\n    /// |-------|-------------|-------------------|\n    /// | -1 | Largest negative value | 1 |\n    /// | -2 | Second largest negative | 2 |\n    /// | -3 | Third largest negative | 3 |\n    /// | -10 | Small negative magnitude | 10 |\n    /// | -100 | Century boundary | 100 |\n    /// | -128 | Power of 2 boundary | 128 |\n    /// | -255 | Byte boundary - 1 | 255 |\n    /// | -256 | Byte boundary | 256 |\n    let test_cases = vec![\n        (-1i64, \"negative one (closest to zero)\"),\n        (-2i64, \"negative two\"),\n        (-3i64, \"negative three\"),\n        (-5i64, \"negative five\"),\n        (-10i64, \"negative ten\"),\n        (-15i64, \"negative fifteen\"),\n        (-16i64, \"negative sixteen (2^4)\"),\n        (-31i64, \"negative thirty-one\"),\n        (-32i64, \"negative thirty-two (2^5)\"),\n        (-63i64, \"negative sixty-three\"),\n        (-64i64, \"negative sixty-four (2^6)\"),\n        (-100i64, \"negative hundred\"),\n        (-127i64, \"negative 127 (2^7 - 1)\"),\n        (-128i64, \"negative 128 (2^7)\"),\n        (-255i64, \"negative 255 (2^8 - 1)\"),\n        (-256i64, \"negative 256 (2^8)\"),\n        (-512i64, \"negative 512 (2^9)\"),\n        (-1000i64, \"negative thousand\"),\n        (-1024i64, \"negative 1024 (2^10)\"),\n    ];\n\n    for (expected_value, description) in test_cases {\n        let yaml = format!(\"value: {}\", expected_value);\n        let value: Result<Value, _> = serde_yaml::from_str(&yaml);\n\n        assert!(\n            value.is_ok(),\n            \"YAML parsing should succeed for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n\n        let value = value.unwrap();\n        let field_value = &value[\"value\"];\n\n        // Verify it's a negative integer\n        assert!(\n            field_value.is_i64(),\n            \"Value should be i64 for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n        let int_value = field_value.as_i64().unwrap();\n        assert_eq!(\n            int_value, expected_value,\n            \"Value should match expected for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n        assert!(int_value < 0, \"Value should be negative for {}\", description);\n\n        // All negative values, even those close to zero, cannot convert to uint32\n        let fits_in_uint32 = int_value >= 0 && int_value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n        assert!(\n            !fits_in_uint32,\n            \"Negative value {} should not fit in uint32 ({})\",\n            expected_value, description\n        );\n\n        // Verify type mismatch error\n        let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"value\", \"uint32\", \"int32_negative\");\n        assert!(\n            error.is_type_mismatch(),\n            \"Type mismatch error should be created for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n\n        let error_msg = format!(\"{}\", error.kind);\n        assert!(\n            error_msg.contains(\"uint32\") || error_msg.contains(\"unsigned\"),\n            \"Error should mention uint32 for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n\n        println!(\"✓ {} ({}): properly rejected as negative\", expected_value, description);\n    }\n\n    println!(\"✓ All maximum negative values (closest to zero) properly handled\");\n}\n\n#[test]\nfn test_negative_one_special_case() {\n    /// Test: -1 as a special boundary case\n    ///\n    /// -1 is particularly important because:\n    /// 1. It's the closest negative integer to zero\n    /// 2. In two's complement, it's all 1s (0xFFFFFFFF for 32-bit)\n    /// 3. When incorrectly cast to unsigned, it becomes UINT32_MAX\n    /// 4. This is a very common bug in C/C++ code\n    let yaml = r#\"value: -1\"#;\n    let value: Result<Value, _> = serde_yaml::from_str(yaml);\n\n    assert!(value.is_ok(), \"YAML parsing should succeed for -1\");\n\n    let value = value.unwrap();\n    let field_value = &value[\"value\"];\n\n    // Verify it's -1\n    assert!(field_value.is_i64(), \"-1 should be parsed as i64\");\n    let int_value = field_value.as_i64().unwrap();\n    assert_eq!(int_value, -1, \"Value should be exactly -1\");\n    assert!(int_value < 0, \"-1 is negative\");\n\n    // Important: -1 does NOT fit in uint32\n    // (Even though some buggy code might incorrectly cast it)\n    let fits_in_uint32 = int_value >= 0 && int_value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n    assert!(\n        !fits_in_uint32,\n        \"-1 should not be considered valid for uint32\"\n    );\n\n    // Verify type mismatch error\n    let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"value\", \"uint32\", \"negative_one\");\n    assert!(\n        error.is_type_mismatch(),\n        \"Type mismatch error should be created for -1\"\n    );\n\n    let error_msg = format!(\"{}\", error.kind);\n    assert!(\n        error_msg.contains(\"uint32\") || error_msg.contains(\"unsigned\"),\n        \"Error should mention uint32/unsigned\"\n    );\n    assert!(\n        error_msg.contains(\"negative\") || error_msg.contains(\"-1\"),\n        \"Error should indicate negative value\"\n    );\n\n    println!(\"✓ -1 properly rejected (critical boundary case)\");\n}\n\n// ============================================================================\n// Zero Boundary Cases\n// ============================================================================\n\n#[test]\nfn test_zero_boundary_transition() {\n    /// Test: Zero boundary and transition from negative to non-negative\n    ///\n    /// This test verifies the behavior at the exact boundary between\n    /// negative and non-negative values.\n    ///\n    /// # Test Cases\n    ///\n    /// | Input | Sign | Can be uint32? | Description |\n    /// |-------|------|----------------|-------------|\n    /// | -1 | Negative | NO | Last negative value |\n    /// | 0 | Zero | YES | First true, \"exact zero (boundary)\"),\n        (1i64, \"positive\", true, \"first positive after zero\"),\n    ];\n\n    for (value, sign_description, should_fit_uint32, description) in test_cases {\n        let yaml = format!(\"value: {}\", value);\n        let parsed: Result<Value, _> = serde_yaml::from_str(&yaml);\n\n        assert!(\n            parsed.is_ok(),\n            \"YAML parsing should succeed for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n\n        let parsed = parsed.unwrap();\n        let field_value = &parsed[\"value\"];\n\n        // Verify it's parsed as integer\n        assert!(\n            field_value.is_i64(),\n            \"Value should be i64 for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n        let int_value = field_value.as_i64().unwrap();\n        assert_eq!(int_value, value, \"Value should match expected\");\n\n        // Check if fits in uint32\n        let fits_in_uint32 = int_value >= 0 && int_value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n        assert_eq!(\n            fits_in_uint32, should_fit_uint32,\n            \"Value {} should {}fit in uint32 ({})\",\n            value,\n            if should_fit_uint32 { \"\" } else { \"not \" },\n            description\n        );\n\n        // For negative values, verify error handling\n        if !should_fit_uint32 {\n            let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"value\", \"uint32\", \"int32_negative\");\n            assert!(\n                error.is_type_mismatch(),\n                \"Type mismatch error should be created for {}\",\n                description\n            );\n\n            let error_msg = format!(\"{}\", error.kind);\n            assert!(\n                error_msg.contains(\"uint32\") || error_msg.contains(\"unsigned\"),\n                \"Error should mention uint32 for {}\",\n                description\n            );\n        }\n\n        println!(\"✓ {}: {} sign, uint32 fit={}\", value, sign_description, fits_in_uint32);\n    }\n\n    println!(\"✓ Zero boundary transition properly handled\");\n}\n\n#[test]\nfn test_zero_with_negative_context() {\n    /// Test: Zero in context where negative was expected\n    ///\n    /// This tests values are properly rejected\n    /// even when their absolute value might be within uint32 range.\n    ///\n    /// The key insight: For uint32, the valid range is [0, 4294967295].\n    /// ANY negative value is invalid, regardless of its magnitude.\n    ///\n    /// # Test Cases\n    ///\n    /// | Input | Absolute Value | Would fit as uint32 | Should Reject? |\n    /// |-------|----------------|-------------------|----------------|\n    /// | -1 | 1 | Yes (as 1) | YES (it's negative!) |\n    /// | -100 | 100 | Yes (as 100) | YES (it's negative!) |\n    /// | -1000 | 1000 | Yes (as 1000) | YES (it's negative!) |\n    /// | -1000000 | 1000000 | Yes | YES (it's negative!) |\n    /// | -1000000000 | 1000000000 | Yes | YES (it's negative!) |\n    let test_cases = vec![\n        (-1i64, 1u64, \"absolute value 1\"),\n        (-100i64, 100u64, \"absolute value 100\"),\n        (-1000i64, 1000u64, \"absolute value 1000\"),\n        (-10000i64, 10000u64, \"absolute value 10000\"),\n        (-100000i64, 100000u64, \"absolute value 100000\"),\n        (-1000000i64, 1000000u64, \"absolute value 1000000\"),\n        (-10000000i64, 10000000u64, \"absolute value 10000000\"),\n        (-100000000i64, 100000000u64, \"absolute value 100000000\"),\n        (-1000000000i64, 1000000000u64, \"absolute value 1000000000\"),\n    ];\n\n    for (negative_value, absolute_value, description) in test_cases {\n        let yaml = format!(\"value: {}\", negative_value);\n        let value: Result<Value, _> = serde_yaml::from_str(&yaml);\n\n        assert!(\n            value.is_ok(),\n            \"YAML parsing should succeed for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n\n        let value = value.unwrap();\n        let field_value = &value[\"value\"];\n\n        // Verify it's a negative integer\n        assert!(\n            field_value.is_i64(),\n            \"Value should be i64 for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n        let int_value = field_value.as_i64().unwrap();\n        assert_eq!(int_value, negative_value, \"Value should be negative\");\n\n        // The absolute value WOULD fit in uint32...\n        assert!(\n            absolute_value <= u32::MAX as u64,\n            \"Absolute value should fit in uint32 for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n\n        // ...but the negative value itself must NOT be accepted\n        let fits_in_uint32 = int_value >= 0 && int_value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n        assert!(\n            !fits_in_uint32,\n            \"Negative value {} should NOT fit in uint32 ({}) - sign matters!\",\n            negative_value, description\n        );\n\n        // Verify type mismatch error\n        let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"value\", \"uint32\", \"int32_negative\");\n        assert!(\n            error.is_type_mismatch(),\n            \"Type mismatch error should be created for {}\",\n            description\n        );\n\n        println!(\n            \"✓ {} ({}): negative sign correctly prevents conversion\",\n            negative_value, description\n        );\n    }\n\n    println!(\"✓ All negative values properly rejected (sign check works correctly)\");\n}\n\n// ============================================================================\n// Summary and Coverage Verification\n// ============================================================================\n\n#[test]\nfn test_int32_to_uint32_boundary_coverage_summary() {\n    /// Test: Verify comprehensive coverage of all boundary conditions\n    ///\n    /// This test verifies that we have covered all the critical boundary\n    /// conditions for int32 to uint32 negative conversion.\n    ///\n    /// # Coverage Checklist\n    ///\n    /// - [x] int32::MIN (-2147483648) - absolute minimum\n    /// - [x] Maximum negative values (-1, -2, -3, etc.) - closest to zero\n    /// - [x] Zero boundary case (transition from -1 to 0 to 1)\n    /// - [x] Range tests across all magnitudes\n    /// - [x] Power-of-2 boundaries\n    /// - [x] Common negative constants\n    /// - [x] Values that would fit as unsigned but are negative\n    ///\n    /// This test documents the coverage and serves as a checklist for\n    /// future maintenance.\n\n    // Document key boundary values\n    let critical_values = vec![\n        (\"int32::MIN\", -2147483648i64),\n        (\"int32::MIN + 1\", -2147483647i64),\n        (\"near int32::MIN\", -2000000000i64),\n        (\"mid negative range\", -1000000000i64),\n        (\"large negative\", -1000000i64),\n        (\"medium negative\", -100000i64),\n        (\"small negative\", -1000i64),\n        (\"tiny negative\", -100i64),\n        (\"negative one\", -1i64),\n        (\"zero\", 0i64),\n        (\"positive one\", 1i64),\n    ];\n\n    println!(\"\\n=== Int32 to Uint32 Boundary Coverage Summary ===\\n\");\n\n    for (description, value) in critical_values {\n        let is_negative = value < 0;\n        let fits_uint32 = value >= 0 && value <= u32::MAX as i64;\n        let should_reject = is_negative;\n\n        println!(\n            \"{}: {} -> negative={}, fits_uint32={}, should_reject={}\",\n            description, value, is_negative, fits_uint32, should_reject\n        );\n\n        // Verify the logic is correct\n        if should_reject {\n            assert!(\n                !fits_uint32 || value < 0,\n                \"Rejected value should either not fit or be negative: {} ({})\",\n                value, description\n            );\n        }\n    }\n\n    println!(\"\\n✓ All boundary conditions documented and verified\");\n    println!(\"✓ Coverage checklist complete\");\n    println!(\"\\nKey Takeaways:\");\n    println!(\"1. ALL negative int32 values are invalid for uint32 conversion\");\n    println!(\"2. Zero (0) is valid - it's the boundary between valid/invalid\");\n    println!(\"3. int32::MIN (-2147483648) is the extreme edge case\");\n    println!(\"4. -1 is the 'closest' negative to zero, but still invalid\");\n    println!(\"5. Magnitude doesn't matter - sign is the critical factor\");\n}\n","numLines":858,"startLine":1,"totalLines":858}}}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_676bae7a6bec4dc5a2093226","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t//! Error types for YAML parsing\n2\t//!\n3\t//! This module defines the error types used throughout the YAML parser.\n4\t//!\n5\t//! # Error Handling Philosophy\n6\t//!\n7\t//! The `ParseError` type follows a structured approach to error handling that emphasizes:\n8\t//!\n9\t//! 1. **Clear categorization** - Each error falls into a distinct category (syntax, I/O, validation, etc.)\n10\t//! 2. **Rich context** - Errors carry location information, context messages, and code snippets\n11\t//! 3. **Composability** - Errors propagate cleanly through the call stack using Rust's `?` operator\n12\t//! 4. **User-friendly output** - Multiple formatting options for different use cases (logging, UI, debugging)\n13\t//!\n14\t//! ## When to Use Each Variant\n15\t//!\n16\t//! ### `ParseErrorKind::Io` vs Other Variants\n17\t//!\n18\t//! Use [`ParseErrorKind::Io`] for **file system and I/O operation failures**:\n19\t//! - File not found (`std::io::ErrorKind::NotFound`)\n20\t//! - Permission denied (`std::io::ErrorKind::PermissionDenied`)\n21\t//! - Read/write failures (`std::io::ErrorKind::Other`)\n22\t//! - Network I/O errors (if applicable)\n23\t//!\n24\t//! **Do not use** `Io` for:\n25\t//! - YAML syntax errors → use [`ParseErrorKind::Syntax`]\n26\t//! - Type mismatches → use [`ParseErrorKind::TypeMismatch`]\n27\t//! - Constraint violations → use [`ParseErrorKind::Validation`]\n28\t//!\n29\t//! ```ignore\n30\t//! // ✅ Correct: Use Io for file system errors\n31\t//! let content = std::fs::read_to_string(path)?;  // io::Error → Io\n32\t//!\n33\t//! // ❌ Incorrect: Don't use Io for parsing errors\n34\t//! // let error = ParseError::io(\"invalid YAML structure\");  // Wrong! Use Syntax instead\n35\t//! ```\n36\t//!\n37\t//! ### `ParseErrorKind::InvalidUtf8` vs Other Variants\n38\t//!\n39\t//! Use [`ParseErrorKind::InvalidUtf8`] specifically for **encoding errors**:\n40\t//! - Invalid UTF-8 byte sequences in input\n41\t//! - Failed UTF-8 validation when converting bytes to strings\n42\t//!\n43\t//! **Do not use** `InvalidUtf8` for:\n44\t//! - I/O errors during file reading → use [`ParseErrorKind::Io`]\n45\t//! - General parsing failures → use [`ParseErrorKind::Syntax`]\n46\t//!\n47\t//! ```ignore\n48\t//! // ✅ Correct: Use InvalidUtf8 for encoding errors\n49\t//! let string = String::from_utf8(bytes)?;  // FromUtf8Error → InvalidUtf8\n50\t//!\n51\t//! // ❌ Incorrect: Don't use InvalidUtf8 for file reading errors\n52\t//! // let error = ParseError::new(ParseErrorKind::InvalidUtf8);  // Wrong! Use Io instead\n53\t//! ```\n54\t//!\n55\t//! ### `ParseErrorKind::UnexpectedEof` vs `ParseErrorKind::Syntax`\n56\t//!\n57\t//! Use [`ParseErrorKind::UnexpectedEof`] when **input ends prematurely**:\n58\t//! - Incomplete YAML documents (missing closing brackets, braces, quotes)\n59\t//! - Truncated files or streams\n60\t//! - Multi-document YAML streams that end mid-document\n61\t//!\n62\t//! Use [`ParseErrorKind::Syntax`] for **general YAML syntax violations**:\n63\t//! - Invalid indentation\n64\t//! - Invalid escape sequences\n65\t//! - Malformed scalars or mappings\n66\t//! - Any YAML grammar violation that's not specifically EOF-related\n67\t//!\n68\t//! ```ignore\n69\t//! // ✅ Correct: Use UnexpectedEof for incomplete input\n70\t//! if input.ends_with(\"key: \") {\n71\t//!     return Err(ParseError::new(ParseErrorKind::UnexpectedEof));\n72\t//! }\n73\t//!\n74\t//! // ✅ Correct: Use Syntax for general YAML errors\n75\t//! if !is_valid_indentation(line) {\n76\t//!     return Err(ParseError::syntax(\"invalid indentation\"));\n77\t//! }\n78\t//! ```\n79\t//!\n80\t//! ### `ParseErrorKind::TypeMismatch` vs `ParseErrorKind::Validation`\n81\t//!\n82\t//! Use [`ParseErrorKind::TypeMismatch`] when **a value has the wrong Rust/YAML type**:\n83\t//! - Expecting an integer but finding a string\n84\t//! - Expecting a sequence but finding a scalar\n85\t//! - Expecting a boolean but finding a number\n86\t//!\n87\t//! Use [`ParseErrorKind::Validation`] for **semantic constraint violations**:\n88\t//! - Value out of allowed range (e.g., port number > 65535)\n89\t//! - String doesn't match required pattern (e.g., invalid email format)\n90\t//! - Array length constraints violated\n91\t//! - Business logic or schema validation failures\n92\t//!\n93\t//! The key distinction: `TypeMismatch` is about **type**, `Validation` is about **value**.\n94\t//!\n95\t//! ```ignore\n96\t//! // ✅ Correct: Use TypeMismatch for type errors\n97\t//! let port = value[\"port\"].as_i64()\n98\t//!     .ok_or_else(|| ParseError::type_mismatch(\"port\", \"integer\", \"string\"))?;\n99\t//!\n100\t//! // ✅ Correct: Use Validation for value constraints\n101\t//! if port < 1 || port > 65535 {\n102\t//!     return Err(ParseError::validation(\"port must be between 1 and 65535\"));\n103\t//! }\n104\t//! ```\n105\t//!\n106\t//! ### `ParseErrorKind::DuplicateKey` vs `ParseErrorKind::Validation`\n107\t//!\n108\t//! Use [`ParseErrorKind::DuplicateKey`] specifically for **duplicate key errors**:\n109\t//! - YAML mappings with repeated keys (YAML 1.2 spec violation)\n110\t//! - Configuration files with conflicting entries\n111\t//!\n112\t//! Use [`ParseErrorKind::Validation`] for **general validation failures** including:\n113\t//! - Missing required fields (not duplicated, but absent)\n114\t//! - Inter-field constraint violations (e.g., \"start_time > end_time\")\n115\t//! - Schema validation failures beyond type/duplicate checking\n116\t//!\n117\t//! ```ignore\n118\t//! // ✅ Correct: Use DuplicateKey for duplicate keys\n119\t//! if keys.contains(&key) {\n120\t//!     return Err(ParseError::new(ParseErrorKind::DuplicateKey(key)));\n121\t//! }\n122\t//!\n123\t//! // ✅ Correct: Use Validation for missing required fields\n124\t//! if value[\"name\"].is_null() {\n125\t//!     return Err(ParseError::validation(\"missing required field: 'name'\"));\n126\t//! }\n127\t//! ```\n128\t//!\n129\t//! ### `ParseErrorKind::Other` (Catch-all) vs Specific Variants\n130\t//!\n131\t//! Use [`ParseErrorKind::Other`] **only as a last resort** for errors that don't fit other categories:\n132\t//! - Unclassifiable serde_yaml errors\n133\t//! - Errors from external libraries that don't map cleanly\n134\t//! - Temporary error cases during refactoring\n135\t//!\n136\t//! **Prefer specific variants** whenever possible:\n137\t//! - Unknown anchors → [`ParseErrorKind::UnknownAnchor`]\n138\t//! - Syntax errors → [`ParseErrorKind::Syntax`]\n139\t//! - I/O errors → [`ParseErrorKind::Io`]\n140\t//! - Type errors → [`ParseErrorKind::TypeMismatch`]\n141\t//!\n142\t//! If you find yourself using `Other` frequently, consider adding a new specific variant.\n143\t//!\n144\t//! ```ignore\n145\t//! // ✅ Prefer specific variants\n146\t//! return Err(ParseError::syntax(\"invalid YAML\"));\n147\t//!\n148\t//! // ⚠️ Use Other only as a catch-all\n149\t//! return Err(ParseError::new(ParseErrorKind::Other(\"unclassified error\".to_string())));\n150\t//! ```\n151\t//!\n152\t//! # Error Propagation Strategy\n153\t//!\n154\t//! The `ParseError` type is designed to work seamlessly with Rust's error propagation\n155\t//! mechanisms. It implements `From` for common error types, enabling the use of the `?`\n156\t//! operator for automatic error conversion.\n157\t//!\n158\t//! ## Basic Propagation with `?`\n159\t//!\n160\t//! ```ignore\n161\t//! use armor::parsers::yaml::{ParseError, Result};\n162\t//!\n163\t//! fn parse_config(path: &Path) -> Result<Config> {\n164\t//!     let content = std::fs::read_to_string(path)?;  // io::Error → ParseError\n165\t//!     parse_yaml(&content)\n166\t//! }\n167\t//! ```\n168\t//!\n169\t//! ## Adding Context with `.context()`\n170\t//!\n171\t//! Use the builder-style `.context()` method to add contextual information:\n172\t//!\n173\t//! ```ignore\n174\t//! fn parse_database_config(value: &serde_yaml::Value) -> Result<DatabaseConfig> {\n175\t//!     let port = value[\"port\"]\n176\t//!         .as_i64()\n177\t//!         .ok_or_else(|| ParseError::type_mismatch(\"port\", \"integer\", \"null\"))\n178\t//!         .context(\"while parsing database configuration\")?;\n179\t//!\n180\t//!     Ok(DatabaseConfig { port })\n181\t//! }\n182\t//! ```\n183\t//!\n184\t//! ## Converting from Other Error Types\n185\t//!\n186\t//! `ParseError` implements `From` for:\n187\t//! - `std::io::Error` → [`ParseErrorKind::Io`]\n188\t//! - `serde_yaml::Error` → Appropriate [`ParseErrorKind`] based on error classification\n189\t//! - `std::str::Utf8Error` → [`ParseErrorKind::InvalidUtf8`]\n190\t//! - `std::string::FromUtf8Error` → [`ParseErrorKind::InvalidUtf8`]\n191\t//!\n192\t//! ## Custom Error Creation\n193\t//!\n194\t//! For domain-specific errors, create them directly:\n195\t//!\n196\t//! ```ignore\n197\t//! fn validate_port(port: i64) -> Result<u16> {\n198\t//!     if port < 1 || port > 65535 {\n199\t//!         return Err(ParseError::validation(\"port must be between 1 and 65535\"));\n200\t//!     }\n201\t//!     Ok(port as u16)\n202\t//! }\n203\t//! ```\n204\t//!\n205\t//! # Examples\n206\t//!\n207\t//! ## Basic Error Creation\n208\t//!\n209\t//! Creating errors using convenience constructors:\n210\t//!\n211\t//! ```\n212\t//! use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n213\t//!\n214\t//! // Create a syntax error\n215\t//! let syntax_err = ParseError::syntax(\"invalid YAML indentation\");\n216\t//! assert!(syntax_err.is_syntax());\n217\t//!\n218\t//! // Create a validation error\n219\t//! let validation_err = ParseError::validation(\"port must be between 1 and 65535\");\n220\t//! assert!(validation_err.is_validation());\n221\t//!\n222\t//! // Create a type mismatch error\n223\t//! let type_err = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"port\", \"integer\", \"string\");\n224\t//! assert!(type_err.is_type_mismatch());\n225\t//! assert_eq!(type_err.summary(), \"<unknown>: type mismatch at 'port': expected integer, got string\");\n226\t//! ```\n227\t//!\n228\t//! ## Error Propagation with `?`\n229\t//!\n230\t//! Using the `?` operator to automatically convert errors:\n231\t//!\n232\t//! ```\n233\t//! use armor::parsers::yaml::{ParseError, Result};\n234\t//! use std::fs;\n235\t//!\n236\t//! fn read_config(path: &str) -> Result<String> {\n237\t//!     // io::Error is automatically converted to ParseError via From impl\n238\t//!     let content = fs::read_to_string(path)?;\n239\t//!     Ok(content)\n240\t//! }\n241\t//!\n242\t//! // This function demonstrates automatic error conversion\n243\t//! fn parse_config_size(path: &str) -> Result<usize> {\n244\t//!     let content = read_config(path)?;  // ParseError propagates via ?\n245\t//!     Ok(content.len())\n246\t//! }\n247\t//!\n248\t//! // Example: successful read\n249\t//! assert!(parse_config_size(\"/dev/null\").is_ok());\n250\t//! ```\n251\t//!\n252\t//! ## Custom Error Handling with Builder Pattern\n253\t//!\n254\t//! Using builder methods to add context and location information:\n255\t//!\n256\t//! ```\n257\t//! use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n258\t//!\n259\t//! fn validate_service_config(yaml_content: &str) -> Result<(), ParseError> {\n260\t//!     // Simulating an error during validation\n261\t//!     if yaml_content.contains(\"port: abc\") {\n262\t//!         let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"service.port\", \"integer\", \"string\")\n263\t//!             .with_path(\"config/services.yaml\")\n264\t//!             .with_line(5)\n265\t//!             .with_column(10)\n266\t//!             .with_context(\"while validating service configuration\")\n267\t//!             .with_snippet(\"services:\\n  - name: web\\n    port: abc\");\n268\t//!\n269\t//!         return Err(error);\n270\t//!     }\n271\t//!     Ok(())\n272\t//! }\n273\t//!\n274\t//! // Create an error to verify the builder pattern\n275\t//! let error = ParseError::validation(\"invalid port\")\n276\t//!     .with_path(\"config.yaml\")\n277\t//!     .with_line(10)\n278\t//!     .with_column(5)\n279\t//!     .with_context(\"while parsing configuration\");\n280\t//!\n281\t//! assert_eq!(error.path, Some(\"config.yaml\".to_string()));\n282\t//! assert_eq!(error.line, Some(10));\n283\t//! assert_eq!(error.column, Some(5));\n284\t//! assert_eq!(error.context, \"while parsing configuration\");\n285\t//! ```\n286\t//!\n287\t//! ## Error Display and Formatting\n288\t//!\n289\t//! Different ways to format and display errors:\n290\t//!\n291\t//! ```\n292\t//! use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n293\t//!\n294\t//! // Create an error with full context\n295\t//! let error = ParseError::syntax(\"unexpected colon\")\n296\t//!     .with_path(\"config/database.yaml\")\n297\t//!     .with_line(15)\n298\t//!     .with_column(8)\n299\t//!     .with_context(\"while parsing database configuration\")\n300\t//!     .with_snippet(\"database:\\n  host: localhost\\n  port: 5432:\\n    invalid\");\n301\t//!\n302\t//! // Get a single-line summary (good for logging)\n303\t//! let summary = error.summary();\n304\t//! assert!(summary.contains(\"config/database.yaml:15\"));\n305\t//! assert!(summary.contains(\"syntax error\"));\n306\t//! assert!(summary.contains(\"while parsing database configuration\"));\n307\t//!\n308\t//! // Get a detailed multi-line report (good for user display)\n309\t//! let detailed = error.detailed_report();\n310\t//! assert!(detailed.contains(\"error:\"));\n311\t//! assert!(detailed.contains(\"snippet:\"));\n312\t//!\n313\t//! // Get a structured representation (good for debugging)\n314\t//! let structured = error.format_structured();\n315\t//! assert!(structured.contains(\"ParseError\"));\n316\t//! assert!(structured.contains(\"config/database.yaml:15:8\"));\n317\t//! assert!(structured.contains(\"line: Some(15)\"));\n318\t//!\n319\t//! // Display implementation provides user-friendly output\n320\t//! let display_string = format!(\"{}\", error);\n321\t//! assert!(display_string.contains(\"config/database.yaml\"));\n322\t//! assert!(display_string.contains(\"syntax error\"));\n323\t//! ```\n324\t//!\n325\t//! ## Error Conversion from Standard Types\n326\t//!\n327\t//! Automatic conversion from standard library errors:\n328\t//!\n329\t//! ```\n330\t//! use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n331\t//! use std::io;\n332\t//!\n333\t//! // io::Error converts to ParseError::Io\n334\t//! let io_err = io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::NotFound, \"file not found\");\n335\t//! let parse_err: ParseError = io_err.into();\n336\t//! assert!(parse_err.is_io());\n337\t//! assert!(parse_err.summary().contains(\"I/O error\"));\n338\t//! assert!(parse_err.summary().contains(\"file not found\"));\n339\t//!\n340\t//! // String::from_utf8 Error converts to ParseError::InvalidUtf8\n341\t//! let invalid_bytes = b\"\\xff\\xfe\\xfd\";\n342\t//! let utf8_err = String::from_utf8(invalid_bytes.to_vec()).unwrap_err();\n343\t//! let parse_err: ParseError = utf8_err.into();\n344\t//! assert!(matches!(parse_err.kind, armor::parsers::yaml::ParseErrorKind::InvalidUtf8));\n345\t//! ```\n346\t//!\n347\t//! ## Working with Error Types\n348\t//!\n349\t//! Checking error types and handling specific cases:\n350\t//!\n351\t//! ```\n352\t//! use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n353\t//!\n354\t//! let errors = vec![\n355\t//!     ParseError::syntax(\"invalid token\"),\n356\t//!     ParseError::io(\"file not found\"),\n357\t//!     ParseError::validation(\"port out of range\"),\n358\t//!     ParseError::type_mismatch(\"field\", \"string\", \"integer\"),\n359\t//! ];\n360\t//!\n361\t//! // Check specific error types\n362\t//! assert!(errors[0].is_syntax());\n363\t//! assert!(errors[1].is_io());\n364\t//! assert!(errors[2].is_validation());\n365\t//! assert!(errors[3].is_type_mismatch());\n366\t//!\n367\t//! // Filter errors by type\n368\t//! let syntax_errors: Vec<_> = errors.iter()\n369\t//!     .filter(|e| e.is_syntax())\n370\t//!     .collect();\n371\t//! assert_eq!(syntax_errors.len(), 1);\n372\t//! ```\n373\t\n374\tuse std::fmt;\n375\t\n376\t/// Main error type for YAML parsing operations\n377\t#[derive(Clone)]\n378\tpub struct ParseError {\n379\t    /// The kind of error that occurred\n380\t    pub kind: ParseErrorKind,\n381\t    /// The line number where the error occurred (1-indexed)\n382\t    pub line: Option<usize>,\n383\t    /// The column number where the error occurred (1-indexed)\n384\t    pub column: Option<usize>,\n385\t    /// The file or source path where the error occurred\n386\t    pub path: Option<String>,\n387\t    /// A code snippet showing the problematic segment\n388\t    pub snippet: Option<String>,\n389\t    /// Additional context about the error\n390\t    pub context: String,\n391\t}\n392\t\n393\timpl ParseError {\n394\t    /// Create a new ParseError with the given kind\n395\t    ///\n396\t    /// This is the base constructor for creating a ParseError. All fields except\n397\t    /// `kind` are set to their default values (None or empty). Use the builder methods\n398\t    /// to add additional context and location information.\n399\t    ///\n400\t    /// # Examples\n401\t    ///\n402\t    /// ```\n403\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::{ParseError, ParseErrorKind};\n404\t    ///\n405\t    /// let error = ParseError::new(ParseErrorKind::UnexpectedEof);\n406\t    /// assert!(matches!(error.kind, ParseErrorKind::UnexpectedEof));\n407\t    /// assert_eq!(error.line, None);\n408\t    /// ```\n409\t    ///\n410\t    /// For common error types, prefer using the convenience constructors:\n411\t    /// - [`ParseError::syntax()`] for syntax errors\n412\t    /// - [`ParseError::io()`] for I/O errors\n413\t    /// - [`ParseError::validation()`] for validation errors\n414\t    /// - [`ParseError::type_mismatch()`] for type mismatch errors\n415\t    pub fn new(kind: ParseErrorKind) -> Self {\n416\t        Self {\n417\t            kind,\n418\t            line: None,\n419\t            column: None,\n420\t            path: None,\n421\t            snippet: None,\n422\t            context: String::new(),\n423\t        }\n424\t    }\n425\t\n426\t    /// Set the line number for this error\n427\t    ///\n428\t    /// Line numbers are 1-indexed, matching typical text editor conventions.\n429\t    ///\n430\t    /// # Examples\n431\t    ///\n432\t    /// ```\n433\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n434\t    ///\n435\t    /// let error = ParseError::syntax(\"invalid token\")\n436\t    ///     .with_line(42);\n437\t    /// assert_eq!(error.line, Some(42));\n438\t    /// ```\n439\t    pub fn with_line(mut self, line: usize) -> Self {\n440\t        self.line = Some(line);\n441\t        self\n442\t    }\n443\t\n444\t    /// Set the column number for this error\n445\t    ///\n446\t    /// Column numbers are 1-indexed, indicating the character position within the line.\n447\t    ///\n448\t    /// # Examples\n449\t    ///\n450\t    /// ```\n451\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n452\t    ///\n453\t    /// let error = ParseError::syntax(\"invalid token\")\n454\t    ///     .with_column(15);\n455\t    /// assert_eq!(error.column, Some(15));\n456\t    /// ```\n457\t    pub fn with_column(mut self, column: usize) -> Self {\n458\t        self.column = Some(column);\n459\t        self\n460\t    }\n461\t\n462\t    /// Set the file/source path for this error\n463\t    ///\n464\t    /// The path can be absolute or relative. It's displayed in error messages to help\n465\t    /// users locate the source of the error.\n466\t    ///\n467\t    /// # Examples\n468\t    ///\n469\t    /// ```\n470\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n471\t    ///\n472\t    /// let error = ParseError::syntax(\"invalid YAML\")\n473\t    ///     .with_path(\"config/services.yaml\");\n474\t    /// assert_eq!(error.path, Some(\"config/services.yaml\".to_string()));\n475\t    /// ```\n476\t    pub fn with_path(mut self, path: impl Into<String>) -> Self {\n477\t        self.path = Some(path.into());\n478\t        self\n479\t    }\n480\t\n481\t    /// Set the code snippet for this error\n482\t    ///\n483\t    /// The snippet should contain the relevant lines of source code where the error occurred.\n484\t    /// This is displayed in detailed error reports to help users understand the context.\n485\t    ///\n486\t    /// # Examples\n487\t    ///\n488\t    /// ```\n489\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n490\t    ///\n491\t    /// let error = ParseError::syntax(\"invalid port value\")\n492\t    ///     .with_line(5)\n493\t    ///     .with_snippet(\"service:\\n  name: web\\n  port: abc\");\n494\t    ///\n495\t    /// let report = error.detailed_report();\n496\t    /// assert!(report.contains(\"service:\"));\n497\t    /// assert!(report.contains(\"port: abc\"));\n498\t    /// ```\n499\t    pub fn with_snippet(mut self, snippet: impl Into<String>) -> Self {\n500\t        self.snippet = Some(snippet.into());\n501\t        self\n502\t    }\n503\t\n504\t    /// Set the context message for this error\n505\t    ///\n506\t    /// Context provides additional information about what operation was being performed\n507\t    /// when the error occurred. This helps users understand the broader scenario.\n508\t    ///\n509\t    /// # Examples\n510\t    ///\n511\t    /// ```\n512\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n513\t    ///\n514\t    /// let error = ParseError::validation(\"port out of range\")\n515\t    ///     .with_context(\"while parsing database configuration\");\n516\t    ///\n517\t    /// let summary = error.summary();\n518\t    /// assert!(summary.contains(\"while parsing database configuration\"));\n519\t    /// ```\n520\t    pub fn with_context(mut self, context: impl Into<String>) -> Self {\n521\t        self.context = context.into();\n522\t        self\n523\t    }\n524\t\n525\t    /// Set both line and column for this error\n526\t    ///\n527\t    /// Convenience method for setting location with a single call.\n528\t    ///\n529\t    /// # Examples\n530\t    ///\n531\t    /// ```\n532\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n533\t    ///\n534\t    /// let error = ParseError::syntax(\"invalid token\")\n535\t    ///     .with_location(10, 25);\n536\t    /// assert_eq!(error.line, Some(10));\n537\t    /// assert_eq!(error.column, Some(25));\n538\t    /// assert_eq!(error.location_string(), \"10:25\");\n539\t    /// ```\n540\t    pub fn with_location(mut self, line: usize, column: usize) -> Self {\n541\t        self.line = Some(line);\n542\t        self.column = Some(column);\n543\t        self\n544\t    }\n545\t\n546\t    /// Get a formatted location string (e.g., \"file.yaml:10:5\" or \"<unknown>:10\")\n547\t    ///\n548\t    /// This method generates a human-readable location string based on the available\n549\t    /// location information (path, line, column). The format adapts based on which fields are set.\n550\t    ///\n551\t    /// # Examples\n552\t    ///\n553\t    /// ```\n554\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n555\t    ///\n556\t    /// // Full location with path, line, and column\n557\t    /// let error = ParseError::syntax(\"test\")\n558\t    ///     .with_path(\"config.yaml\")\n559\t    ///     .with_location(10, 5);\n560\t    /// assert_eq!(error.location_string(), \"config.yaml:10:5\");\n561\t    ///\n562\t    /// // Line and column only\n563\t    /// let error = ParseError::syntax(\"test\")\n564\t    ///     .with_location(42, 15);\n565\t    /// assert_eq!(error.location_string(), \"42:15\");\n566\t    ///\n567\t    /// // Path only\n568\t    /// let error = ParseError::syntax(\"test\")\n569\t    ///     .with_path(\"config.yaml\");\n570\t    /// assert_eq!(error.location_string(), \"config.yaml\");\n571\t    ///\n572\t    /// // Unknown location\n573\t    /// let error = ParseError::syntax(\"test\");\n574\t    /// assert_eq!(error.location_string(), \"<unknown>\");\n575\t    /// ```\n576\t    pub fn location_string(&self) -> String {\n577\t        match (&self.path, self.line, self.column) {\n578\t            (Some(path), Some(line), Some(col)) => format!(\"{}:{}:{}\", path, line, col),\n579\t            (Some(path), Some(line), None) => format!(\"{}:{}\", path, line),\n580\t            (Some(path), None, Some(col)) => format!(\"{}::{}\", path, col),\n581\t            (Some(path), None, None) => path.clone(),\n582\t            (None, Some(line), Some(col)) => format!(\"{}:{}\", line, col),\n583\t            (None, Some(line), None) => format!(\"{}\", line),\n584\t            (None, None, Some(col)) => format!(\"col {}\", col),\n585\t            (None, None, None) => \"<unknown>\".to_string(),\n586\t        }\n587\t    }\n588\t\n589\t    /// Get a brief summary of the error (single line, suitable for logging)\n590\t    ///\n591\t    /// The summary includes location, error kind, and context (if set). This is ideal\n592\t    /// for logging purposes where a compact, single-line error message is desired.\n593\t    ///\n594\t    /// # Examples\n595\t    ///\n596\t    /// ```\n597\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n598\t    ///\n599\t    /// // Summary with context\n600\t    /// let error = ParseError::syntax(\"invalid token\")\n601\t    ///     .with_path(\"config.yaml\")\n602\t    ///     .with_line(10)\n603\t    ///     .with_context(\"while parsing services\");\n604\t    /// assert_eq!(error.summary(),\n605\t    ///     \"config.yaml:10: syntax error: invalid token - while parsing services\");\n606\t    ///\n607\t    /// // Summary without context\n608\t    /// let error = ParseError::syntax(\"invalid token\")\n609\t    ///     .with_path(\"config.yaml\")\n610\t    ///     .with_line(10);\n611\t    /// assert_eq!(error.summary(),\n612\t    ///     \"config.yaml:10: syntax error: invalid token\");\n613\t    /// ```\n614\t    pub fn summary(&self) -> String {\n615\t        let location = self.location_string();\n616\t        if !self.context.is_empty() {\n617\t            format!(\"{}: {} - {}\", location, self.kind, self.context)\n618\t        } else {\n619\t            format!(\"{}: {}\", location, self.kind)\n620\t        }\n621\t    }\n622\t\n623\t    /// Get a detailed multi-line error report with snippet and visual indicator\n624\t    ///\n625\t    /// This method generates a comprehensive error report suitable for displaying\n626\t    /// to users. It includes the error summary, context (if set), and a code snippet\n627\t    /// with a visual indicator (^) pointing to the error location.\n628\t    ///\n629\t    /// # Examples\n630\t    ///\n631\t    /// ```\n632\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n633\t    ///\n634\t    /// let error = ParseError::syntax(\"invalid port value\")\n635\t    ///     .with_path(\"config.yaml\")\n636\t    ///     .with_line(5)\n637\t    ///     .with_column(10)\n638\t    ///     .with_context(\"while parsing service configuration\")\n639\t    ///     .with_snippet(\"service:\\n  port: abc\");\n640\t    ///\n641\t    /// let report = error.detailed_report();\n642\t    /// assert!(report.contains(\"error:\"));\n643\t    /// assert!(report.contains(\"config.yaml:5:10\"));\n644\t    /// assert!(report.contains(\"syntax error: invalid port value\"));\n645\t    /// assert!(report.contains(\"while parsing service configuration\"));\n646\t    /// assert!(report.contains(\"snippet:\"));\n647\t    /// ```\n648\t    pub fn detailed_report(&self) -> String {\n649\t        let mut report = String::new();\n650\t\n651\t        // Header line with location and error kind\n652\t        report.push_str(&format!(\"error: {}\\n\", self.summary()));\n653\t\n654\t        // Add context section if present\n655\t        if !self.context.is_empty() {\n656\t            report.push_str(&format!(\"  context: {}\\n\", self.context));\n657\t        }\n658\t\n659\t        // Add snippet with visual indicator\n660\t        if let Some(snippet) = &self.snippet {\n661\t            report.push_str(\"\\n  snippet:\\n\");\n662\t            for line in snippet.lines() {\n663\t                report.push_str(&format!(\"    {}\\n\", line));\n664\t            }\n665\t\n666\t            // Add visual indicator for column position\n667\t            if let Some(col) = self.column {\n668\t                if col > 0 {\n669\t                    report.push_str(&format!(\"    {}^\\n\", \" \".repeat(col.saturating_sub(1))));\n670\t                }\n671\t            }\n672\t        }\n673\t\n674\t        report\n675\t    }\n676\t\n677\t    /// Format this error as a structured log entry (JSON-like but readable)\n678\t    ///\n679\t    /// This method produces a structured representation of the error suitable for\n680\t    /// programmatic analysis or structured logging systems.\n681\t    ///\n682\t    /// # Examples\n683\t    ///\n684\t    /// ```\n685\t    /// use armor::parsers::yaml::ParseError;\n686\t    ///\n687\t    /// let error = ParseError::type_mismatch(\"port\", \"integer\", \"string\")\n688\t    ///     .with_path(\"config.yaml\")\n689\t    ///     .with_line(8)\n690\t    ///     .with_column(10);\n691\t    ///\n692\t    /// let formatted = error.format_structured();\n693\t    /// assert!(formatted.contains(\"ParseError\"));\n694\t    /// assert!(formatted.contains(\"config.yaml:8:10\"));\n695\t    /// assert!(formatted.contains(\"line: Some(8)\"));\n696\t    /// assert!(formatted.contains(\"column: Some(10)\"));\n697\t    /// ```\n698\t    pub fn format_structured(&self) -> String {\n699\t        format!(\n700\t            \"ParseError {{ kind: {:?}, location: {}, line: {:?}, column: {:?} }}\",\n701\t            self.kind,\n702\t            self.location_string(),\n703\t            self.line,\n704\t            self.column\n705\t        )\n706\t    }\n707\t\n708\t    /// Create a syntax error\n709\t    pub fn syntax(msg: impl Into<String>) -> Self {\n710\t        Self::new(ParseErrorKind::Syntax(msg.into()))\n711\t    }\n712\t\n713\t    /// Create an I/O error\n714\t    pub fn io(msg: impl Into<String>) -> Self {\n715\t        Self::new(ParseErrorKind::Io(msg.into()))\n716\t    }\n717\t\n718\t    /// Create a validation error\n719\t    pub fn validation(msg: impl Into<String>) -> Self {\n720\t        Self::new(ParseErrorKind::Validation(msg.into()))\n721\t    }\n722\t\n723\t    /// Create a type mismatch error\n724\t    ///\n725\t    /// # Arguments\n726\t    /// * `field` - The field path where the error occurred\n727\t    /// * `expected` - The expected type description\n728\t    /// * `actual` - The actual type that was received\n729\t    pub fn type_mismatch(field: impl Into<String>, expected.into(),\n733\t            actual: actual.into(),\n734\t        })\n735\t    }\n736\t\n737\t    /// Check if this is a syntax error\n738\t    pub fn is_syntax(&self) -> bool {\n739\t        matches!(self.kind, ParseErrorKind::Syntax(_))\n740\t    }\n741\t\n742\t    /// Check if this is an I/O error\n743\t    pub fn is_io(&self) -> bool {\n744\t        matches!(self.kind, ParseErrorKind::Io(_))\n745\t    }\n746\t\n747\t    /// Check if this is a validation error\n748\t    pub fn is_validation(&self) -> bool {\n749\t        matches!(self.kind, ParseErrorKind::Validation(_))\n750\t    }\n751\t\n752\t    /// Check if this is a type mismatch error\n753\t    pub fn is_type_mismatch(&self) -> bool {\n754\t        matches!(self.kind, ParseErrorKind::TypeMismatch { .. })\n755\t    }\n756\t}\n757\t\n758\timpl fmt::Display for ParseError {\n759\t    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\n760\t        // Use the summary method for concise display\n761\t        write!(f, \"{}\", self.summary())?;\n762\t\n763\t        // Add detailed section with snippet if available\n764\t        if let Some(snippet) = &self.snippet {\n765\t            write!(f, \"\\n\\n  snippet:\")?;\n766\t            for line in snippet.lines() {\n767\t                write!(f, \"\\n    {}\", line)?;\n768\t
